Sorry. Uh, tax grew to 116.6 billion last year. This is reflective of a successful year from land revenue as we've delivered our core business of collecting and distributed money and implementing the government's tax and social policy work program. Maintaining the integrity of the tax system remains critical, and we have strong systems and processes in place to support this.

Where mistakes or unintended errors occur, we take them seriously and focus on delivering solutions quickly and in the most appropriate ways. Over the past year, inland revenue has continued to build on the strong technology foundations and the new systems processes and people capabilities that business transformation enabled.

We acknowledge that the rate of revenue growth has flattened compared to previous years, a reflection of the broader economic conditions, which some individuals and businesses have found challenging. We've continued to make it easier and simpler to get things right and hard to get things wrong with 94.5% of our main taxes being income tax.

GST and PAYE paid on time. This is significant and shows the current approach to encouraging and supporting voluntary compliance with a broad-based tax system is largely successful. While a number of people who don't do the right thing is relatively small, their actions have a significant impact on those around them.

And we have a strong focus on tackling tax debt, which reached 9.3 billion in 2025. In the last year, 4.3 billion in overdue tax and entitlement debt was collected. The investment in our compliance activities of 61.5 million per annum across budgets 24 and 25 is being used to good effect.

We've significantly increased our audit and enforcement activities and focused on high risk sectors, including property non-compliance, trusts, organized crime, and the hidden economy. We're also making use of data and insights in new ways to better target our interventions. We're seeing positive results with the return on investments from compliance interventions reaching $11.80 for every dollar spent against this year's target of $10.

This compares with $9 50 for the 2324 year. We've also delivered a range of government priorities, including Family Boost and more recently investment boost. We're actively making better use of the digital tools available to us, which enable us to be more efficient and effective. Part of this includes keeping pace with technology, and we're working to understand the opportunities digital tools like AI provide for us and our customers, as well as how it might be used by other parties who might, might seek to disrupt our business.

In terms of the public service, we make a broader contribution through engaging in partnerships and information sharing where it can deliver greater value and better results. Examples include contributing to the all of government focus on digitizing public services and being part of the multi-agency effort to combat organized crime.

We also partnered with the Ministry of Education to deliver Family Boost, and we have more than 60 information sharing agreements with over 20 government agencies. All of our work has been delivered while making savings to our baseline appropriation, managing cost pressures and self-funding, some policy initiatives to meet the government's focus on reducing public sector costs.

This has led us to look for more innovative ways to do things and improve service delivery. Our people have continued to work hard, which has resulted in us meeting or exceeding our targets in many areas. I'm really proud of their dedication and commitment, coupled with a respectful and empathetic approach to those who need our help as we work together to deliver a tax and social policy system for all New Zealanders.
